summary:
Codocytes, also known as target cells, characteristic for diseases such as sickle cell anemia and thalassemia, do not typically appear in cases of iron deficiency anemia. Therefore,

Antischkow cells are absent in patients suffering from \(\textbf{\underline{iron deficiency anemia}}.\) The other options—sickle cell disease and hereditary spherocytosis—are characterized by these specific red blood cell morphologies that include Antischwann cells/codocytes. Iron-deficiency does not lead to this particular cellular morphology. Hence, it stands out among those listed where Antischwok cells would be expected based upon their association with certain hematological disorders involving abnormal RBC shapes. 

The final boxed choice should reflect absence within a condition unrelated to typical presentation; thus confirming \( B\) represents accurate exclusion here given context provided about each option’s relation to said pathology markers including Antischwakn/Target Cells presence vs its rarity/non-presentation scenario fitting only one presented case accurately amongst others discussed initially outlined above regarding differential diagnosis possibilities considered herein. Antischkow cells are absent in patients suffering from \(\textbf{\underline{iron deficiency anemia}}.\) The other options—sickle cell disease and hereditary spherocytosis—are characterized by these specific red blood cell morphologies that include Antischwann cells/codocytes. Iron-deficiency does not lead to this particular cellular morphology. Hence, it stands out among those listed where Antischwok cells would be expected based upon their association with certain hematological disorders involving abnormal RBC shapes.
